failure_surface: Formula in target cells produces incorrect results due to invalid end-of-month date calculation and improper handling of multi-value criteria range. Additionally, code execution syntax errors prevented proper implementation.
root_cause: The agent constructed a formula using DATE() with hardcoded day '31' for end-of-month boundary, which creates invalid dates for months with fewer than 31 days. Furthermore, SUMIFS does not accept a range as a single criterion for matching multiple values - this requires SUMPRODUCT with ISNUMBER(MATCH(...)) instead. Finally, the code execution repeatedly failed due to syntax errors where function-call wrapper strings were improperly formatted or embedded in code blocks.
minimal_fix_hypothesis: When computing end-of-month boundaries dynamically, derive it from the first day of the following month minus one day rather than hardcoding day 31. When matching against multiple criteria values from a range, verify whether the Excel function supports array criteria; if not (e.g., SUMIFS), use alternative formulas like SUMPRODUCT combined with MATCH.
evidence: Formula uses DATE(YEAR($F$5),MONTH($F$5),31) which fails for months with ≤30 days
Original instruction explicitly states formula 'not producing expected results' especially for criteria from a list/range
Multiple SyntaxError entries show function_call wrapper strings contaminated Python code execution
SUMIFS cannot take a range ($F$10:$F$14) as a single criterion parameter - requires different formula approach

memory_items.title: End-of-Month Date Boundary Calculation
memory_items.lesson: Never hardcode day number 31 when computing month-end dates. Always calculate the last day by finding the first day of the next month and subtracting one day.
memory_items.generalization: For any date range calculation involving month boundaries, compute end_date = start_date.replace(day=1) + timedelta(days=month_length) - timedelta(days=1) or use built-in functions like EOMONTH if available.

memory_items.title: Multi-Value Criteria in Conditional Aggregation Functions
memory_items.lesson: Excel functions like SUMIFS do not support passing a range as a single criterion to match multiple possible values. Use SUMPRODUCT with ISNUMBER(MATCH(criterion_range, lookup_array, 0)) pattern instead.
memory_items.generalization: Before implementing array criteria logic, verify the target function's documented capabilities. When matching against multiple discrete values from a list/range, prefer SUMPRODUCT+MATCH over SUMIFS.

memory_items.title: Execution Hygiene for Spreadsheet Automation Scripts
memory_items.lesson: Ensure generated code blocks are clean Python without wrapper string contamination. Function call delimiters should not appear within executable code content.
memory_items.generalization: Validate code before execution: check that no meta-formatting characters, JSON-like wrappers, or template markers exist inside the actual code string passed to the interpreter.

edits.content:
## Critical Pattern Lessons

### Date Range Boundaries
- Never hardcode day '31' for month-end calculations. Some months have 28-31 days.
- Compute end-of-month as: first_day_of_next_month minus one day.
- Use built-in Excel functions (EOMONTH) or programmatic date libraries for reliability.

### Array Criteria Limitations
- Functions like SUMIFS accept only single-value criteria per argument pair.
- For matching against multiple values from a range/list, use SUMPRODUCT with ISNUMBER(MATCH(...)).
- Verify function documentation before implementing multi-criteria logic.

### Code Execution Safety
- Ensure generated code is pure Python without wrapper contamination.
- Test small code snippets independently before full workbook operations.
- Handle SyntaxErrors immediately by reviewing string formatting and escape sequences.
